content.text: Event Storming is a collaborative workshop technique that brings domain experts, developers, and product managers together to map out complex workflows through shared discovery. When applied to generative AI and agentic systems, it becomes a powerful tool for surfacing the events, commands, policies, and actors that an AI agent must understand and handle — replacing vague feature descriptions with concrete, actionable capability definitions.
What It Is

Event Storming, introduced by Alberto Brandolini, is a structured workshop format in which participants place colour-coded sticky notes on a long paper roll or digital whiteboard to model the events, commands, systems, and actors that make up a business domain. The process deliberately begins without a predefined schema — participants throw events on the board freely, then the facilitator guides the group through successive refinement passes that add temporal order, commands, policies, read models, and bounded contexts.

In the context of generative AI feature development, the technique is adapted to target a different class of domain events: the conversation turns, tool invocations, agentic decisions, failure modes, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints that define what an AI feature must do. Rather than modelling an e-commerce order lifecycle, the workshop surfaces events such as "user query received," "agent plan generated," "external API called," "response graded insufficient," and "escalation to human triggered." Each of these becomes a candidate capability, acceptance criterion, or risk item in the product backlog.

The session output is not a UML diagram or a formal specification — it is a shared understanding encoded in a physical artefact (the sticky-note board) and a set of downstream artefacts: bounded contexts that map to microservices or agent modules, aggregate roots that map to stateful agent components, and hotspot stickers that flag unresolved risks or knowledge gaps requiring further investigation before implementation begins.
Why It Matters

GenAI features are notoriously hard to specify in advance. Unlike CRUD features where requirements can be expressed as database schemas and REST endpoints, AI capabilities involve probabilistic behaviour, emergent failure modes, and strong dependency on LLM context windows, tool reliability, and user intent. Teams that skip structured discovery and jump straight to prompting often discover that their agents handle the happy path but break in dozens of edge cases that were never anticipated.

Event Storming forces the team to walk through the entire event timeline — including failures, retries, ambiguous inputs, and policy enforcement moments — before a single line of code is written. This surfaces capability gaps ("we never discussed how the agent handles a tool timeout"), scope disagreements ("the product manager assumed the agent would answer follow-up questions; the engineer assumed it would not"), and safety blind spots ("what happens when the agent is asked to perform an irreversible action?"). Discovering these issues during a two-hour workshop is orders of magnitude cheaper than discovering them during production incidents.
Benefits
Shared vocabulary creation
— participants walk away with agreed definitions for key terms such as "agent," "tool," "observation," and "human checkpoint," reducing the ambiguity that accumulates in async communication.
Failure mode discovery
— the workshop's negative-event stickers (marked with red hotspot dots) force the team to enumerate what can go wrong before the optimistic path is fully drawn.
Bounded context identification
— the clusters of events that belong together reveal the natural seams between agent sub-systems (query understanding, tool orchestration, response synthesis), guiding microservice boundaries.
Stakeholder alignment
— involving product, engineering, legal, and operations in a single room surfaces the policy and compliance constraints that engineering-only discovery sessions miss.
Direct backlog seeding
— each identified aggregate or policy becomes a potential user story, and each hotspot becomes a spike or risk mitigation task.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Requires domain experts who understand both AI behaviour and the business problem — if only engineers attend, the session models implementation rather than user-facing capabilities.

GenAI events are probabilistic, not deterministic — the workshop produces a map of possible event flows rather than a guaranteed execution trace, which can mislead attendees into treating the board as a specification.
Time-intensive — a thorough event storming session for a complex agent feature takes three to six hours, plus a facilitation preparation phase of one to two hours.
The technique originates in traditional domain-driven design and may need explanation before participants only familiar with agile story mapping can participate productively.
Digital tooling (Miro, Mural) loses the tactile immediacy of physical stickies and can reduce the spontaneous rearrangement that happens naturally when participants physically cluster notes.
How It Works

The classic Event Storming format unfolds in five progressive phases, each building on the previous. The facilitator's role is to guide transitions, prevent premature consensus, and maintain the energy needed for participants to keep generating events rather than stopping to debate any single one.
Phase 1: Chaotic Exploration

Participants write domain events on orange sticky notes and place them on the board without any ordering or grouping. The only constraint is that events must be written in past tense ("Query received," "Tool invocation failed," "Response graded insufficient"). The facilitator encourages quantity over quality and explicitly discourages debates. Duplicate events are kept — they signal importance. For GenAI scoping, prompts such as "What does the agent observe?" and "What can go wrong?" help participants surface failure states that are easy to overlook.
Phase 2: Timeline Ordering

The group arranges events in chronological order from left to right. Conflicts and uncertainties are marked with red hotspot stickers rather than resolved immediately. Commands (pink stickies) are added to represent what triggers each event — "Submit query" triggers "Query received." For agent workflows, commands often correspond to API calls, user messages, or scheduled triggers. Policies (yellow stickies) capture the rules that determine which command fires in response to an event: "When tool call fails → Retry up to three times."
Phase 3: Bounded Context Identification

The facilitator draws dashed boxes around clusters of related events. Each box represents a bounded context — a sub-domain with its own consistent vocabulary. In a typical agent system, natural contexts emerge around query understanding, agent orchestration, tool management, response generation, and safety evaluation. These bounded contexts become candidates for separate microservices, agent modules, or code ownership boundaries.
Phase 4: Aggregate Identification

Aggregates (light blue stickies) are the stateful entities that accept commands and emit events. For an agent system, the conversation thread, the active plan, and the tool call registry each qualify as aggregates. Each aggregate has an identity (a session ID, a plan ID), a lifecycle, and a set of invariants it enforces. Identifying aggregates at this stage guides the design of agent state management and persistence.
Phase 5: Capability Backlog Extraction

The final phase converts the board into product artefacts. Each bounded context becomes an epic. Each aggregate becomes a service or module. Each command-event-policy triple becomes a user story or acceptance criterion. Hotspots become spike tasks or risk mitigation items. The resulting backlog is grounded in the domain events the team collaboratively surfaced, making it far more defensible than a requirements list written in isolation.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Event Storming five-phase flow for GenAI capability discovery

content.text: The diagram illustrates the five phases: starting from chaotic orange event stickies, moving through timeline ordering with commands and policies, grouping into bounded contexts, mapping to aggregates, and finally extracting a prioritised capability backlog. Red items represent discovered hotspots — risks and unknowns that require follow-up spikes before development begins.
When to Use It

Event Storming is most valuable at the beginning of a new GenAI feature or agent system, before any architectural decisions are made. Use it when the feature involves multi-step agentic workflows where the sequence of events and decisions is not obvious to all stakeholders, or when multiple teams (product, platform, safety, legal) need to align on the scope and boundaries of the AI capability before development begins. It is also appropriate when integrating a new external LLM or tool and when running post-incident retrospectives to map the event timeline of a production failure.
Event Storming is
not

the right tool when the team needs to deep-dive into a single narrow capability that is already well understood, or when the audience lacks familiarity with the business domain entirely and would benefit more from a customer journey mapping session first.
How to Implement
Identify participants
: Invite 6–12 people covering product, engineering, data science, safety/compliance, and at least one domain expert. Too few participants miss knowledge; too many make facilitation unmanageable.
Prepare the board

: Set up a long horizontal surface (physical paper roll or a Miro/FigJam board with at least 4,000px horizontal space). Prepare coloured sticky templates for events (orange), commands (pink), actors (yellow person icons), systems (blue), policies (yellow text), and hotspots (red dot).
Seed with trigger events

: Place two or three known events to anchor the timeline — typically the first user input event on the far left and the final response event on the far right. This gives participants a frame of reference without constraining the middle.
Run the chaotic exploration phase
: Give participants 20–30 minutes to write as many events as possible. Prompt with: "What does the LLM decide here?" "What external call is made?" "What can fail?" "Who must approve this step?"
Order and cluster
: Guide the group to arrange events on the timeline, place hotspot stickers on conflicts, and add commands and policies. This phase typically takes 45–90 minutes for a complex agent workflow.
Draw bounded contexts and identify aggregates
: The facilitator draws dashed borders around clusters, then the group names aggregates within each context. Each aggregate name becomes a candidate class or service in the architecture.
Extract the backlog

: After the session, the product manager converts each context into an epic, each aggregate into a service story, each hotspot into a spike, and each policy into an acceptance criterion. Photograph or export the board before participants leave.
Examples

The following script demonstrates a digital Event Storming board in code — representing the sticky-note structure as Python dataclasses and running a simple validation pass that checks for missing commands and orphaned events.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class StickyType(Enum):
    EVENT = "event"        # orange
    COMMAND = "command"    # pink
    POLICY = "policy"      # yellow
    SYSTEM = "system"      # blue
    HOTSPOT = "hotspot"    # red
    AGGREGATE = "aggregate" # light-blue

@dataclass
class Sticky:
    id: str
    sticky_type: StickyType
    text: str
    triggered_by: Optional[str] = None
    bounded_context: Optional[str] = None

class EventStormingBoard:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stickies: dict[str, Sticky] = {}

    def add(self, sticky: Sticky) -> None:
        self.stickies[sticky.id] = sticky

    def events(self):
        return [s for s in self.stickies.values() if s.sticky_type == StickyType.EVENT]

    def hotspots(self):
        return [s for s in self.stickies.values() if s.sticky_type == StickyType.HOTSPOT]

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        warnings = []
        for event in self.events():
            if event.triggered_by is None:
                warnings.append(f"Orphan event (no command): {event.text!r}")
            elif event.triggered_by not in self.stickies:
                warnings.append(f"Event {event.text!r} references missing command")
        return warnings

    def grouped_by_context(self) -> dict[str, list[Sticky]]:
        result: dict[str, list[Sticky]] = {}
        for s in self.stickies.values():
            ctx = s.bounded_context or "Unassigned"
            result.setdefault(ctx, []).append(s)
        return result

# Example: agent capability discovery session output
board = EventStormingBoard()

board.add(Sticky("cmd-1", StickyType.COMMAND, "Submit user query"))
board.add(Sticky("cmd-2", StickyType.COMMAND, "Generate execution plan"))
board.add(Sticky("cmd-3", StickyType.COMMAND, "Invoke search tool"))
board.add(Sticky("cmd-4", StickyType.COMMAND, "Synthesise final answer"))

board.add(Sticky("evt-1", StickyType.EVENT, "Query received",
                 triggered_by="cmd-1", bounded_context="Query Context"))
board.add(Sticky("evt-2", StickyType.EVENT, "Execution plan generated",
                 triggered_by="cmd-2", bounded_context="Agent Execution"))
board.add(Sticky("evt-3", StickyType.EVENT, "Search results retrieved",
                 triggered_by="cmd-3", bounded_context="Tool Management"))
board.add(Sticky("evt-4", StickyType.EVENT, "Tool call timed out",
                 triggered_by="cmd-3", bounded_context="Tool Management"))
board.add(Sticky("evt-5", StickyType.EVENT, "Answer synthesised",
                 triggered_by="cmd-4", bounded_context="Response Context"))
board.add(Sticky("evt-6", StickyType.EVENT, "Human escalation triggered",
                 bounded_context="Safety"))  # No command yet — hotspot!

board.add(Sticky("pol-1", StickyType.POLICY, "If tool call timed out → Retry up to 3 times"))
board.add(Sticky("hot-1", StickyType.HOTSPOT, "Who decides when to escalate to human?"))
board.add(Sticky("hot-2", StickyType.HOTSPOT, "What PII filter applies before search call?"))

print("=== Validation ===")
for w in board.validate():
    print(" !", w)

print("\n=== Contexts ===")
for ctx, stickies in board.grouped_by_context().items():
    print(f"[{ctx}]")
    for s in stickies:
        print(f"  {s.sticky_type.value.upper():<12} {s.text}")

print("\n=== Hotspots ===")
for h in board.hotspots():
    print(f"  ! {h.text}")
This second example shows how to convert the Event Storming board output into structured user stories and spike items for a product backlog:
Real-World Example

A fintech company building an AI-powered transaction dispute assistant ran a full-day Event Storming session with 11 participants: two product managers, three engineers, a data scientist, a compliance officer, a fraud analyst, a UX researcher, and two customer service representatives. The goal was to discover the capability scope of an agent that would autonomously handle Tier-1 disputes (those below $500 and matching known fraud patterns).

In the chaotic exploration phase, 87 event stickies were placed in 25 minutes. After timeline ordering, the board revealed three clusters: dispute intake (events around identity verification, transaction data retrieval, and pattern matching), autonomous resolution (events around policy lookup, decision generation, and customer notification), and escalation (events around complexity scoring, human handoff, and case documentation). These became three epics in the next sprint planning session.

Seven hotspots emerged that would not have been discovered without the workshop: the compliance officer flagged that the agent could not access raw transaction data without an explicit PII masking step, the fraud analyst identified two event types that the existing data model did not capture, and the UX researcher noted that the agent's notification event lacked a consent acknowledgement step. Each became a spike task. The session reduced the estimated discovery time from four weeks of async documentation to a single structured day.
Common Mistakes
Writing events as actions rather than past-tense facts
— "Call the API" is a command, not an event. The event is "API response received." Mixing the two types makes the causal structure unreadable.
Skipping failure events

— teams often model only the happy path. For AI agents, failure events ("LLM returned malformed JSON," "Tool call exceeded rate limit") are as important as success events because they drive policy and retry logic.
Resolving hotspots during the session

— the session's purpose is discovery, not decision-making. When a conflict appears, place a hotspot sticker and move on. Attempting to resolve it consumes facilitation time and inhibits continued exploration.
Treating the board as a final specification
— the board is a shared understanding artefact, not a design document. Specific model calls, API shapes, and data schemas are determined downstream in design documents and code.
Running the session without a dedicated facilitator

— when a senior engineer or product manager facilitates while also contributing events, they tend to steer the board toward their existing mental model rather than eliciting the full group's knowledge.
Best Practices

Seed the board with two known endpoint events before participants arrive — the first user-facing trigger and the final outcome. This prevents the paralysis that sometimes occurs when participants face a blank board.

Use GenAI-specific prompts during chaotic exploration: "What decisions does the model make here?" "What external service does the agent depend on?" "What human approval is needed?" "What data is too sensitive to send to the LLM?"
Photograph the board at the end of every phase, not just the final state — intermediate board states often capture discarded ideas that become important later.
Assign a note-taker whose only job is to capture decisions, context, and the reasoning behind hotspot placement. The board captures the what; the notes capture the why.
Schedule a follow-up session 24–48 hours after the initial workshop. Second-pass sessions reliably surface a further 10–20% of events that were missed in the first session.
Use consistent naming conventions for events ("[Noun] [past-tense verb]") so that event names can be directly reused as domain vocabulary in code — class names, log event types, and metric labels.
Performance and Scalability

The Event Storming workshop itself does not have runtime performance concerns, but the quality of the session output directly affects the downstream scalability of the agent architecture. Well-identified bounded contexts lead to independently deployable services that can be scaled horizontally without coordination. Poorly identified contexts lead to monolithic agent implementations where every capability change requires a full redeploy.

For large organisations, the technique scales by running parallel "Big Picture" sessions that map the full system landscape, followed by focused "Design Level" sessions that zoom into a single bounded context with a smaller group. The Big Picture session (typically 6–12 people, 3–4 hours) identifies the top-level contexts; the Design Level session (typically 4–8 people, 2–3 hours per context) produces the detailed event map used for implementation.

The board artefact can become unwieldy when a domain has more than 150–200 events. In practice, this signals that the scope is too large for a single session. Split into two sessions, one per major sub-domain, and use a context map to document how the sub-domains integrate. For AI systems with multi-modal capabilities (text, code, image, audio), run separate sessions per modality and then a joint integration session to identify the handoff events between modalities.

Security and Reliability

Event Storming sessions for AI features should explicitly include a security event storming pass — a second run through the timeline specifically asking "What can an adversarial user do at this event?" and "What sensitive data is present at this point in the flow?" This is distinct from the business-event storming pass and surfaces PII exposure, prompt injection entry points, and privilege escalation paths that would not appear in a purely functional model.

The session artefact (the board and its photographs) often contains sensitive domain information — business logic, compliance rules, and the names of internal systems. Treat the board export as a confidential internal document. For digital boards, restrict access to session participants and use the platform's access control features.

Reliability of the session outcome depends on the completeness of the hotspot resolution process. Every hotspot that is not resolved before implementation begins is a latent reliability risk. Track hotspots as first-class backlog items with owners and due dates. Define a policy that no feature can enter the implementation phase while any hotspot in its bounded context remains unresolved.
Key Takeaways
Event Storming adapts naturally to GenAI discovery by targeting the events, commands, policies, and failure modes specific to LLM and agent-driven workflows.
The five-phase structure (chaos → order → contexts → aggregates → backlog) converts a room of tacit knowledge into a structured product backlog in a single session.
Hotspot stickers are the most valuable output — each one represents a risk that would otherwise silently become a production defect.
Bounded contexts identified in the session map directly to microservice or module boundaries in the implementation, making the workshop a design activity as well as a requirements activity.
A dedicated facilitator (not a participant) is essential — their only job is to maintain flow, ensure all voices contribute, and prevent premature convergence on any single event or decision.
Include a security-focused event storming pass for AI features to surface prompt injection entry points, PII exposure windows, and privilege escalation paths before implementation.
Session output should be digitised and converted to backlog items within 24 hours; shared understanding rapidly degrades if the artefact is not captured and actioned promptly.
